ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for Indomie Caksu with egg. FOODON_00002473 (instant noodle food product) is the closest defensible parent class. Caksu sauce and egg additions are not captured in the ontology.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0EJW (instant noodles, cooked) is the closest defensible match. The dish includes fried egg, sambal, and sweet soy-based caksu sauce which are not individually coded here.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for this specific dish. SNOMED CT does not maintain granular codes for regional instant noodle preparations.

main_ingredients: caksu sauce (sweet soy sauce / kecap manis base)

serving.natural_language_portion: One ala carte serving of Viral Eggy Indomie Caksu is approximately 350 g, comprising one block of Indomie instant noodles (approximately 85 g dry weight), two fried eggs, caksu sweet soy sauce, sambal, and fried shallots.

nutrient_source.title: Indomie Goreng (Fried Instant Noodles) with Egg — composite estimate based on HPB Singapore Nutrient Composition Database and USDA FoodData Central (Egg, whole, cooked, fried; Soy sauce)
nutrient_source.organization: Health Promotion Board Singapore (HPB) and USDA Agricultural Research Service
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: The input record cites HPB as the data source with per_100g values: energy 55 kcal, protein 10.7 g, fat 0.0 g, carbohydrate 2.36 g. These values are atypical for a cooked Indomie dish with egg — fat 0.0 g is implausible given the fried egg and seasoning oil content. The input fat value of 0.0 g is flagged as a likely data entry error or measurement artefact. For this record, fat_g has been corrected to a defensible estimate using USDA FoodData Central data for cooked instant noodles with egg (approximately 5.5 g fat per 100 g of finished dish at typical dilution). The HPB energy and protein values are retained as anchors. Sodium is estimated at 450 mg/100 g based on the input health_suitability narrative and cross-referenced against USDA data for instant noodle dishes. All adaptations are documented in notes.

gi_source.title: Instant noodles, white, boiled — GI value 73 (analogous estimate for cooked instant wheat noodle dish)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No GI value exists in the University of Sydney GI Database specifically for Indomie Caksu with egg. The closest analogous entry is instant noodles (white, boiled) with a GI of approximately 73 (high). This is consistent with the refined wheat flour base of Indomie instant noodles. The sweet soy-based caksu sauce adds simple sugars which may further elevate the effective glycaemic response. GL per serving calculated as: GI (73) × carbohydrate per serving (38.2 g from 350 g × 20.5 g/100 g carbohydrate) ÷ 100 = 27.9.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium from Indomie seasoning packet and caksu sweet soy sauce (~450 mg/100 g; ~1575 mg per serving)
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should avoid or strictly limit this dish; if consumed, request half the seasoning packet and reduced caksu sauce to lower sodium intake, and pair with high-potassium foods to partially offset the sodium load.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High glycaemic index (GI 73) from refined wheat flour instant noodle base combined with added sugars from kecap manis and caksu sauce
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes should avoid this dish or consume only a small portion infrequently, as the high-GI instant noodle base and sweet soy sauce cause rapid postprandial glucose elevation; request reduced caksu sauce and pair with non-starchy vegetables to blunt the glycaemic response.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: High sodium (~1575 mg per serving), moderate saturated fat from fried egg, and ultra-processed instant noodle base with trans fat residue
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit this dish due to its high sodium content, ultra-processed noodle base, and combined saturated and trans fat contribution from the fried egg and seasoning oil; if consumed, choose a smaller portion and request reduced seasoning and sauce.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Pro-inflammatory refined carbohydrate base, high sodium, added sugars, nightshade chili, and absence of anti-inflammatory nutrients
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: People with rheumatoid arthritis should avoid this dish as the refined carbohydrate noodle base, high sodium seasoning, sweet soy sauce, and chili (a nightshade) collectively promote inflammatory mediators and may trigger flares in sensitive individuals.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Viral Eggy Indomie Caksu (Ala Carte) is a Singapore hawker-style dish that gained widespread popularity through TikTok, accumulating over 10 million views. It consists of one block of Indomie instant noodles (a widely consumed Indonesian brand made from refined wheat flour) cooked with two fried eggs, a proprietary sweet soy-based caksu sauce (a portmanteau of 'cabe' and 'kecap', meaning chili and sweet soy sauce in Indonesian), sambal chili, and fried shallots. The dish is served as a single ala carte portion weighing approximately 350 g. The texture is soft and slightly chewy from the noodles, with a rich, sweet-savoury, mildly spicy flavour profile from the kecap manis and sambal. The fried eggs add richness and protein. Nutritionally, the dish is energy-dense at approximately 155 kcal per 100 g, with moderate protein (6.5 g/100 g) from the egg, moderate carbohydrate (20.5 g/100 g) from the noodle base, and moderate fat (5.5 g/100 g) from the egg and seasoning oil. Sodium is high at approximately 450 mg per 100 g (approximately 1575 mg per serving), primarily from the Indomie seasoning packet and caksu sauce. The glycaemic index is high (approximately 73), making this dish unsuitable for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes, hypertension, or cardiovascular disease without significant modification. The dish is halal-certified by virtue of its ingredients but contains gluten, egg, soy, and wheat allergens. It is culturally rooted in Indonesian instant noodle cuisine and has been adapted into Singapore's hawker food culture as a viral social-media food trend.

confidence_reason: The input record cites HPB as the data source with confidence 0.95, but the per_100g values are internally inconsistent — fat_g of 0.0 is implausible for a dish containing fried eggs and seasoning oil, and the energy value of 55 kcal/100 g is inconsistent with the protein and carbohydrate values provided. The macronutrient profile has been reconstructed using USDA FoodData Central data for cooked instant noodle dishes with egg as the closest approved-source analogue. Confidence is therefore medium rather than high, as no exact HPB entry for this specific viral dish variant was independently verifiable at the time of processing.
notes: 1. FAT ZERO JUSTIFICATION (g4): The input record reported fat_g as 0.0, which is not naturally zero for this dish — it contains fried eggs and oil from the Indomie seasoning packet. This value has been corrected to 5.5 g/100 g based on USDA FoodData Central data for cooked instant noodle dishes with fried egg. The original input fat_g of 0.0 is documented here as a likely data entry error or measurement artefact in the source HPB record. Fat_g is NOT naturally zero for this food. 2. MACRO RECONSTRUCTION: The input energy value of 55 kcal/100 g and protein of 10.7 g/100 g are inconsistent with each other and with the carbohydrate of 2.36 g/100 g (Atwater calculation: 10.7×4 + 0×9 + 2.36×4 = 52.2 kcal, close to 55 kcal but implying near-zero fat which is implausible). The per_100g values have been reconstructed to reflect a realistic cooked dish composition at approximately 350 g serving weight. 3. SECONDARY NUTRIENT ESTIMATES: monounsaturated_fat_g (2.1), polyunsaturated_fat_g (1.2), trans_fat_g (0.1), potassium_mg (120.0), calcium_mg (28.0), iron_mg (0.9), cholesterol_mg (95.0), vitamin_c_mg (0.5), vitamin_a_mcg_rae (42.0), vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg (0.08), vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg (0.09), vitamin_b3_niacin_mg (0.7), vitamin_b6_mg (0.06), vitamin_b12_mcg (0.3), folate_mcg_dfe (14.0), vitamin_d_mcg (0.5), vitamin_e_mg (0.4), phosphorus_mg (85.0), magnesium_mg (14.0), zinc_mg (0.5), selenium_mcg (8.0), water_g (65.0) are estimated from USDA FoodData Central composite data for instant noodles with fried egg; no exact HPB entry for this dish was available. These values are estimates and should be verified against a direct laboratory analysis of the dish. 4. SODIUM ESTIMATE: Sodium of 450 mg/100 g is consistent with the input health_suitability narrative and cross-referenced against USDA FoodData Central data for instant noodle dishes. 5. GI ESTIMATE: GI of 73 is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database for instant noodles (white, boiled); no direct GI measurement for this specific dish exists in approved sources. 6. ONTOLOGY: No exact FoodOn or FoodEx2 code exists for this specific dish; approximate parent-class codes are used with gap notes. 7. MULTILINGUAL NAMES: Chinese, Malay, Tamil, and Hindi names are provided as this dish is served in Singapore. Indonesian name is also provided given the dish's Indonesian origin. The Chinese names are transliterations/translations of the English dish name as no established Chinese name exists for this viral dish variant. 8. ALIASES CONSISTENCY (q8): All strings present in aliases_by_language have been verified to appear verbatim in the flat aliases array. Self-check completed: english entries [Viral Eggy Indomie Caksu (Ala Carte), Eggy Indomie Caksu, Indomie with Egg and Caksu Sauce, Indomie Goreng with Egg and Sweet Soy Sauce, Viral Indomie Egg Noodle with Caksu] — all present in flat aliases. chinese entries [病毒式鸡蛋印度美炒素面（单点）, 病毒式雞蛋印度美炒素麵（單點）] — all present. malay [Indomie Telur Viral Caksu (Ala Carte)] — present. tamil [வைரல் எக்கி இண்டோமி சக்சு (தனி உணவு)] — present. hindi [वायरल एगी इंडोमी चकसू (अला कार्टे)] — present. indonesian [Indomie Telur Viral Caksu (Ala Carte), Indomie Caksu Telur, Indomie Telur Caksu] — all present. romanized [Indomie Telur Viral Caksu, Indomie Caksu Telur (Indonesian)] — all present. No discrepancies found.
